detailed_results.question: Which embeddings obtain better results dense or sparse?
detailed_results.expected_answer: Dense embeddings obtain better results than sparse embeddings.
detailed_results.source_passage: Dense embeddings obtain a +5.4 points absolute advantage on the held-out test set.
detailed_results.generated_answer: Based on the provided context, the dense embeddings (Word2Vec) obtain better results compared to the sparse embeddings (TF-IDF). This is evident from the comparison of the test scores in the tables [1], [2], [7], and [8]. In these tables, the dense Word2Vec model consistently achieves higher scores than the sparse TF-IDF model. For example, in table [1], the LR+Word2Vec (dense) model has a test score of 0.910, while the LR+TF-IDF (sparse) model has a test score of 0.856. Similarly, in table [7], the dense Word2Vec model has a test score of 0.910, while the sparse TF-IDF model has a test score of 0.856.

detailed_results.question: What is the model with highest computational cost?
detailed_results.expected_answer: The model with the highest computational cost is the Bert model.
detailed_results.source_passage: While BERT delivers the highest accuracy, its deployment cost is immense: it requires 3.5GB of VRAM and infers significantly slower than the Logistic Regression model.

detailed_results.question: What preprocessing is applied to the texts?
detailed_results.expected_answer: All text is lower-cased, non-alphabetic characters are removed, English stop-words (NLTK) are discarded, and tokens are lemmatised with WordNetLemmatizer.
detailed_results.source_passage: Preprocessing. All text is lower-cased, non-alphabetic characters are removed, English stop-words (NLTK) are discarded, and tokens are lemmatised with WordNetLemmatizer.

detailed_results.question: Which metric is used for evaluation?
detailed_results.expected_answer: The metric used for evaluation is a composite metric created for this task.
detailed_results.source_passage: AN 2025 official scoring scheme, the primary optimisation metric is a composite score: Score = 1/5 AUC+(1−Brier)+F1 +F0.5u +c@1 where c@1rewardsabstention when the system is genuinely uncertain (uncertain band ±0.05 around θ=0.5) [2]. Hyperparam eter grid search is performed over C ∈ {0.01,0.1,1} (sparse) and C ∈ {0.01,0.1,1,10} (dense), all via GridSearchCV.
detailed_results.generated_answer: The LLM took too long to respond. Please try a shorter question.

3(a) XGBoost Feature Importance (b) TabNet Feature Importance
Figure 2: Feature importance graphs
7 Results and Model Comparison
Across all experiments, XGBoost consistently achieved higher PR-AUC, Precision, Recall and
F1-score than TabNet, while also requiring significantly less training time and computational
resources.
Table 1: Model performance comparison
Model PR-AUC Precision Recall F1-score
XGBoost 0.98 1.00 0.96 0.98
TabNet 0.98 0.98 0.95 0.96

13 Monitoring and ObservabilityAproxywascreatedtoconnecttheRenderserverwithlocalPrometheusandGrafanainstances.
Although this setup allowed us to monitor certain internal metrics, such as CPU usage, the
custom metrics described below were not accessible. Therefore, an additional simulation was
conducted using a local API server.
13.1 Data Drift Detection
